response:
Decision recorded in `MEMO.md`.

**Free-tier cap: 300 requests/day per key.**

The 30-day CSV shows per-key daily averages tightly banded between 94 and 106 (mean ~100.5) — nobody is close to the 1,000 the metering rig was built for, and none of the daily numbers even approach 150. That kills Option A (1,000): shipping what the rig happens to support isn't a decision, and at 10x the busiest observed usage it would never function as a conversion lever. It also argues against a cap right at ~150 (Option B as scoped): that sits so close to the mean that a normal busy day for a legitimate hobbyist would trip it, which for a solo-maintained product mostly buys support friction, not signal.

300/day — roughly 3x the highest observed per-key average — gives real users headroom for a busy day while still being low enough that anyone running a recurring or batch workload will cross it within days to weeks, which is the group actually worth converting.

I've also written down concrete triggers to revisit: raise it if people who hit the cap mostly churn rather than upgrade once Pro data exists; lower it if the usage baseline itself drifts up materially; and revisit on a fixed 60-days-after-Pro-launch clock regardless, so the guess gets replaced by real conversion data rather than left in place indefinitely.
